question: Which of the following are true about BASH
options: Bash is a type of Linux kernel
explanation:
bash is most popular Linux shell. A shell is a command interpreter. 

bash is an acronym for born again shell.

All shell commands and file names in Linux are case sensitive.

question: Which of the following statements are true?
options: Any one create and delete a user account.
Only root can create and delete user account
Command to create a new user account is createuser
explanation: A user account can be created or deleted only by the root user.

question: Identify the wrong statement.
options: .. in a file path indicates parent directory
~ in file path indicates home directory of user

question: Is the file a.txt physically deleted after the following set of commands
explanation:
When rm command is used to delete a file, only the hard link is deleted. A file will be physically deleted only after all its links are deleted. 

In this case as the file has two links, after ln command, rm command only removes one link. 

ln a.txt b.txt   - Both a.txt and b.txt show two links

rm a.txt - Now link a.txt is removed and b.txt shows only one link

question: What does the following command do?
options: Changes the name of file a.txt to u+x
explanation:
Only a file which has execute permission can be executed in Linux. Using chmod with +x will give execute permission to the file. 
u+x gives execute permission to user
a+x to all
g+x to group
o+x to others

question: Which command is used to hide a file a.txt in Linux?
explanation:
In Linux, files whose names start with . will be hidden.

So if we rename the file and add . to begining of filename, it becomes hidden.

Hidden files can be displayed by using ls -a command (a for all)

question: What is Linux Kernel
explanation: Core of the OS is the kernel. Command interpreter is shell. Along with kernel and shell there are also applications and utilites in Linux operating system

question: Which command creates a file called a.txt
explanation: touch command creates a file if the file does not exist. It modifies the time stamps of the file, if it exists.

question: What are the contents of /etc/passwd?
options: User names, their home directories, login shells
explanation:
passwd file contains user name, user id, group id, user's home directory and default shell of the user.

When ever a user account is created, /etc/passwd file is updated. 

In modern linux versions, passwd file does not contain user passwords.

question: Which command gives a description about Linux commands?
explanation: Both man and info give description about commands and various options. whatis command gives very short description of the command.

question: What does who command do?
options: Gives all users currently logged in
Gives more information about the website owner
explanation: who command displays all users logged to the system. whoami displays user name of current user.

question: Which environment variable gives exit status of previous command?
Which vi command displays line numbers?
How do you save and exit the vi editor?
explanation:
In command mode
:w - saves the file
:wq - saves and quits
:q! - quits without saving

question: The name of linux kernel is ----
explanation: The linux kernel vmlinuz will be stored in /boot directory.

question: The command to change password of a user in Linux is -----
explanation:
passwd command can be used by a normal user to change his/her password. the command can be used by root user(or using su or sudo) to change password of any user

passwd
or 
passwd username

question: How do you display hidden files in Linux
Which command is used to list the processes running ?
Which command is used to count the lines and words of a file?
The command used to display the name of working directory is ---
The command to search for  a pattern within a file is -----
explanation:
grep or globally search a regular expression and print is used for searching pattern in files. 

grep "NoDisplay" /etc/xdg/autostart/*
shows lines from all files in the directory /etc/xdg/autostart which contain the regular expression "NoDisplay"

question: When a new user is created the files and directories in ------ are copied automatically.
explanation: System administrator can store the common structure for new users in this directory.

question: Which command deletes all the contents of working directory including the sub-directories?
explanation: The flag -r (recursive) removes the sub-directories and their contents as well.

question: Which command in vi editor deletes a single charcter
explanation:
x - delete a single character
dd - delete one line
:q! quit the editor without saving

question: Which command in vi editor cuts one or multiple lines?
explanation: dd cuts the lines and places them in a buffer. These can be pasted using p command

question: Which command in vi editor pastes one or multiple lines?
explanation:
yy yanks the lines (copies the lines without cutting them) and p pastes these lines.  
yy
3yy
yw - yank to the start of next word

question: Which of these commands is not a directory command?
explanation: ps lists the running processes

question: Which directory contains configurations files in Linux
explanation: /bin and /sbin contain executable files. /var contains the third party applications and library files

question: Which keys will close the terminal?
explanation: ctrl d terminates any command.

question: What does whatis command do?
options: Browses the web for given key word
Displays one line information about a linux command
explanation: man and info commands give complete description of given commands. whatis gives a very short description of a command

question: The three permissions for a file are
explanation:
A file can have a total 9 permissions - read , write and execute permissions for owner of the file, group he belongs to and others. 

The permissions can be modified by using chmod command.

Syntax is 
chmod ugoa+-rwx filename

Any   of u(user owner), g(group), o(others) and a(all) can be used with any of three permissions.

Some examples are

chmod g-rw myfile.txt
chmod og+r abc.1
chmod u+x myscript.sh
chmod a+x myscript.sh

question: The executable files in Linux have -----
explanation: Unlike in Windows operating system, in Linux OS the file name extension for executable files is not necessary. Any file can be made by giving execute permission to it.

question: The command which starts a basic calculator in Linux is
The command chmod a-w a.txt -----
options: Makes the file read and append only
explanation:
a - all
-w = remove write permission

So the command removes write permission for all users. And thus makes the file read only

question: The command to search for string "if" in vi editor is -------
explanation:
/followed by text will search for that text in vi editor forwards
n will search for the next occurance of search text forwards.

?followed by search string will search text in backwards. N will search next occurance of search text backwards

question: The command to display the lines of a file in ascending order is ----
explanation: sort command sorts the lines of a file in alphabetical order. The command does not modify the original file - but just displays sorted text on screen

question: The command uname ------
options: Gives the name of working directory
explanation: uname command gives the os name. uname -a gives linux kernel version, processor architecture, the machine name

question: The command used to terminate a process is ---
explanation:
kill -9 followed by process id terminates the process. -9 is for force terminate.

kill is used to send any signal to the process 
Another way to terminate a process is to give the command pkill process-name

question: The command ls>myfile -------
options: lists all files in directory myfile
copies the output of ls command to myfile
explanation: > is a redirection operator. It sends the output of a comamnd to the file specified. (< is used for input redirection and >> is used for append)

question: The command ls>>myfile -------
options: copies the output of ls command to myfile
Appends the output of ls command to myfile

question: What does the command ls|sort do?
options: Copies output of ls command to a file called sort
Sorts the output of ls command
explanation:
| is a pipe operator. It sends the output of one command as input to another command. 

In this case output of ls command is sent as input to sort command. And the file list is displayed sorted.

question: The command to display first 10 lines of a file a.txt is -----
explanation:
head will display first 10 lines of a file. head followed b -n will display first n lines of a file.
To display last n lines, you can use tail command.

question: What does environment variable $HOME contain?
options: Path of home directory of user

question: All the commands and file names in Linux are case sensitive. True or false?
explanation:
In Linux OS, commands and filenames are case sensitive.
e.g.
 a.txt, A.txt, A.TXT, a.TXT are all different files. The command to display file list is ls , not LS or Ls.

question: What does the command file do?
options: Displays the content of a file
Displays the type of file e.g. executable, directory, socket, device etc.
Copies the file to parent directory
explanation:
file command is used to display type of file. 

e.g.

file aapt
aapt: ELF 64-bit LSB shared object, x86-64, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2, for GNU/Linux 2.6.24, not stripped

question: Which of the following is not a shell initialization file?
explanation:
.profile and .bashrc are user specific initial scripts for the bash. They are stored in user's home directory.
/etc/profile and /etc/bash.bashrc are generic initial scripts for all users.

question: To create an alias "dir" for ls command, we have to use------
The command to print all enviroment variables is -----
In a shell script the variable $1 is -----
explanation:
In a shell script $1,$2 etc are called positional parameters and are first, second etc parameters supplied to the shell script.

e.g.
./myshellscript 10 22

In this script file $1 will be taken as 10 and $2 as 22

$# will be total number of parameters
$@ will be the complete set of parameters.

question: Which character in the file path represents the home directory of user?
explanation:
~ represents user's home directory

~/mycodes/a.java

will be /home/username/mycodes/a.java

where username is the name of current user.

question:
Which files does the command 

rm a[123].txt

delete?
explanation: In the command the regular expression [123] means any of 3 characters 1,2 or 3

question:
What does the command 

sort abc.txt

do?
options: Sorts the file and saves it back
Only displays the file in sorted order
explanation:
sort command sorts the lines of file and displays it. To save this sorted content, you need to use -o option and output file name

e.g.
sort one.c -o onesorted.c

question: The character > is used to ------
options: Redirect the output of a command to a file
Redirect output of a command to input of another command
explanation:
> redirects output to file
< redirects input from a file
| pipes output of one command as input to another command

question: The character >> is used to ------
options: Redirect the output of a command to a file
Redirect output of a command to input of another command
Append the output of a command to a file

question: To run a command in background, you need to-----
options: Add a & to the end of command
Add # to the end of command
explanation:
A command followed by & will run the command in the background. 

To bring the process to foreground again, we have to use the command fg followed by optional task name.
e.g.
firefox&
Firefox is running in background and your command prompt is ready for you.
fg

Now firefox is running in foreground and command prompt is waiting for it to terminate.

question: To send output of one command as input to another command, we have to use -----
explanation:
| (pipe) takes output of first command and sends it as input to second command. 

e.g.

ls -l|sort

ps|wc

question: To auto complete a command in bash, you can use --- key
explanation: tab key completes commands and filenames. If there are multiple options, it shows us

question: Which of the following is not a valid run level?
explanation:
Linux system can start in the following 7 run levels.

0 - halt
1 - single user text mode
2 - not used (user definable)
3 - multi user text mode(CLI)
4 - not used (user definable)
5 - multiuser graphical mode (GUI)
6 - reboot

To find what run level you are logged on to, you can use the command 
runlevel

question: When the linux system is booted, which file is loaded into memory?
explanation: kernel file which is named vmlinuz is loaded when a linux system boots. This file is located in /boot directory

question: Single user mode in booting runs in ----- mode
explanation: Using recovery options, you can boot into single user mode. This will log you into root user.

question: Which process has the process id of 1?
explanation: init process has the process id of 1. This is the ancestor of all processes.

question: How do you bring a background process to foreground?
explanation: The process which is in background can be brought to foreground using fg command.

question: How does a user run a command as a root user?
explanation:
Using su or sudo, one can run a command as root user. 

When you use su command, you are prompted for root password. Once it is provided, you will switch to root's shell. You can run the sequence of commands needed and terminate root shell by using "exit" command. 

su can also be used for switching to some other user. 

e.g.
su John
will ask John's password and switch to John's shell.

sudo prompts for user's own password and executes one single command with root privileges.

question: To send a signal to a process, you must use ------
explanation:
kill command is used to send signal to a running process.

syntax is
kill -signalnumber processid

kill -SIGTERM 1599

will send signal sigterm to the process id 1599

To forcibally kill a process, you should use kill -9 processid. 
-9 is SIGKILL

question: To terminate a process, you should use ---
explanation:
SIGTERM signal sent to a process may immediately terminate the process or terminate the process after cleaning up the resources.
In some cases, the process may ignore SIGTERM and continue to run indefinitely.

SIGKILL can never be ignored. Sending SIGKILL will terminate the process immediately. 

If the process is zombie or in uninterruptible sleep, then sending SIGKILL may not kill it.

question: The /proc directory is a virtual directory which contains -----
options: new file system where you can store processes
explanation: /proc is a virtual file system which has process information. Each running process has an subdirectory entry in the /proc directory. Each sub-directory of these processes has process informations like command, memory,

question: The nice values assigned to processes are in the range
explanation:
The NICENESS of a process is its priority. Higher the nice value, lower is the priority.

The NICE value of a process can be changed using 
renice -n nicevalue pid

Nicevalue can be from -20 to 19. 

To start a new command with a nice value, you can use 
nice nicevalue command
e.g.
nice -1 gedit

The default nice value of processes is 0

question: The command ps displays
options: All prcoesses belonging to current terminal and current EUID
explanation:
ps without any parameters displays only to processes running in the current terminal/virtual terminal

ps -e 
or 
ps -A
displays all running processes.

ps -ef displays running processes in full format

question: To dispay every process running in the system, we have to add the option  -----  to ps command
Which of the following file systems allows fast file system recovery?
Which file system is automatically mounted during boot?
explanation:
the entries found in /etc/fstab are automatically mounted after boot. 

Each entry has information about file system, its mount point, its type etc.

question: The command used to mount a file read only is ----
explanation:
A file system is also mounted in read only mode by giving option ro as 
mount -o ro

question: How do you remove read, write and execute permission to others to a file a.txt
explanation:
In chmod + grants permission, - removes permissions.

The letters o,g and u stand for others, group and user(owner) of the file.

e.g.
To add execute permission to user and group, you can use 
chmod ug+x file.sh

To remove write permissions to group and others, you can use

chmod go-w file.sh

question: The command to change the owner of a file a.txt to alex, the command is -----
explanation:
chown can modify the group of a file too.

e.g.
chown alex::alex myfile

Sets the owner and group of the file myfile to alex.

question: What does the command chmod +t do?
What permissions are given to the group after the command chmod 0644 a.txt?
explanation:
chmod followed by an number specifies permissions as bits in the number. The bits are as follows.

type user group others
-       rwx    rwx   rwx

r, w and x represent read, write and execute permssion. The first group of rwx represents permissions for the user owner of the file. Second rwx represents permissions for group the user belongs to. And the third rwx is for permission for others. 

e.g.

chmod 0777 a.txt
indicates the permission 
0111 111 111
So the file a.txt has read,write and execute permission for everyone. 

chmod 0640 b.txt
means 0110 100 100
So b.txt has read and write permission to user owner. Read permission for the group and no permission for others. 

The permissions of a file can also be changed using 
u/g/o/a+/-r/w/x

chmod u+x filename
chmod u+rwx filename
chmod o-rwx filename
chmod g-r filename

etc.

question: Hard link increases the link count of a file. True or false?
explanation:
A hard link is filename refererring to an inode. There can be multiple file names referrring to a single inode. Each such filename is a link to the file. 

When we list files using -l flag, we see number of links to the file in second column. 

A file will be physically deleted when all its hard links are deleted.

question: Which directory contains device special files?
explanation:
In linux every thing is a file. Devices are also files.

/dev/input/mouse0 is mouse device. 

/dev/sda1 is your disk device

question: Which of the following is not a valid file type in Linux?
explanation: inode is a structure which has all information about a file, except for filename.

question: The two types of device files are -----
explanation: In fact, there are 5 types of files in Linux - character, block, pipe, symbolic link and socket files.

question: Which of the following is an example of block device file?
How many links are created when a directory is created?
explanation: When a directory is created, it has two links - one to its parent directory and another to itself.

question: Which command is used to rename a file?
The command cd .. changes the working directory to ------
explanation: .. in  a file path indicates parent directory.

question: The command to display a file, one page at a time is --------
explanation:
Both more or less display the file conents one page at a time. less command lets you to scroll backwards and forwards. But more lets you to scroll only forwards.

You can even give the command
more filename
or
less filename

to show the contents of a file one page at a time.

question: How do you execute pwd command within vi editor
How do you determine the path of an executable file?
explanation:
which followed by executable command shows you which version of the file is being executed.

e.g. which ls
/bin/ls

question: The character used to comment a line in shell script is ------
What does the following script do?
options: Prints Hello followed by current user name.
explanation:
$USER is an environment variable which gives the user name of current user.

When we use an environment variable in a echo statement, its value is printed.

question:
In the script below
   #!/bin/bash
   a = 10
   ---------

to print the value of "a" using echo statement, you must use
explanation:
echo a 
will display a character a.

$a stands for the variable a and hence it must be used in echo statement here.

question: In a script file, $1 and $2 are ------
explanation:
$1, $2, $3 etc are called positional parameters and they represent the first, second, third etc parameters sent to the script when running it.

e.g.
./myscript.sh 10 3 Janice

Here 10 is first parameter i.e. $1, 3 is $2 and Janice is $3

@# gives the total number of positional parameters and $@ gives the complete set of parameters.

question:
The command

  echo {D,Dr}avid
will print ----
explanation: TODO check info and add here

question:
The command 
echo $@
in  a shell script will print -----
explanation:
$@ gives the complete set of all positional parameters

#!/bin/bash
echo "$1 is \$1"
echo "$2 is \$2"
echo "all parameters is $@"


If we run this script as

./myscript.sh   Hello Henry Welcome

the output will be
Hello is $1
Henry is $2
all parmaeters is Hello Henry Welcome

question: The characters back ticks (`) are used in a script for
explanation:
Any command between backticks is evaluated and its output is used in the command

#!/bin/bash
b=1
a=`echo $b`
echo "a is $a"

The output will be a is 1

the command echo $b is evaluated and its output which is 1 is set to a.

question:
The command 
    echo `ls` 
will display
options: List of all files in current directory
explanation: As ls is in back ticks, it is evaluated and its output is displayed by echo command.

question: What does the following script display?
explanation:
An arithmetic expression can be written within two pairs of brackets preceded by $ sign.

Here $(($a+12)) will evaluate a+12 and return 22

question: What does the following script print?
qncode:
#!/bin/bash
a=10
b=30
if [ $a > $b ]; then
  echo "$a is greater"
else
  echo "$b is greater"
fi
explanation:
A test is written within square brackets and uses different operators. 

In this case as a is not greater than b, else part is executed and b is printed.

Do not forget to add a semicolon after test condition.

question: What does the following script print?
qncode:
#!/bin/bash
a=10
b=30
if [$a > $b]; then
  echo "$a is greater"
else
  echo "$b is greater"
fi
explanation:
For [] in testing conditions, there must be a space after [ and a space before ]

As the script does not have spaces adjacent to square brackets, it produces an error.

question: What does the script given below do?
qncode:
#!/bin/bash
a=10
b=30
if [ -a "four.sh" ]; then
  echo "TRU"
fi
options: Tests whether the file is appendable
Tests whether the file is read only
explanation: -a or -e before file name can be used to test whether the file exists.

question: What does the script shown do?
qncode:
#!/bin/bash
for file in `ls`
do
   cat $file
done
options: Displays names of all files in working directory
Displays contents of all files in working directory
explanation: `ls` does the command substitution and returns list of all files. This list as taken for for loop and for loop prints the content of each file by executing cat $file command.

question: Complete the following code to print numbers from 1 to 9
Complete the script to test whether the file is executable. The file must be given as command line parameter
qncode:
#!/bin/bash
-----------------
   echo "It is executable  file"
else
   echo "It is not executable"
fi
explanation:
-x tests whether the file has execute permission or not.

As we need the file to be given as parameter, we should use $1 for filename - which will be the parameter we send while executing the script.

e.g.

./filetest.sh one.c

Now the script checks if one.c has execute permission.

question: What character is used to redirect the output of a command to a file?
explanation:
> is used for redirection of output to a file. 
< is used for redirecting input
>> is used for appending output of a command to a file.

question: The command used to display inode number of a file "a.txt" is ---
explanation: -i flag in ls command displays the inode number

question:
The command

find ~/ -name "*.png"
will
options: Search for all png files in working directory.
Search for png files in user's home directory
Search for png file in users home directory and all sub-directories recursively
explanation:
find command searches for filename patterns hierarchically.
e.g.

will search for filenames with extension sh in root directory and all subdirectories recursively.

question: During linux boot process, what happens immediately after loading kernel?
What command is used to copy a directory along with all its child contents?
explanation: cp with flag -r or -R will copy subdirectories also recursively.

question: The command uname -r displays
explanation:
uname command displays various informations about the system. 

uname -s will print kernel name

uname -m will print machine hardware name
uname -v will print kernel version

uname -a
will print all informations about the system.

question: The command to display the groups you are member of is -----
explanation:
groups will display your groups.

The command groups followed by username will print the groups of that user.

The group names and its member names are stored in the file /etc/group

question: To continue to run a command in background even after logging out, you should use ----
explanation:
nohup is a command to ignore the hup(hangup) signal. Hangup signal is given by tty to child processes when it is being closed.

To run a command, let us say gedit and continue to use it even after the terminal is closed, you can use the command 

nohup gedit

question: To know if two files are hardlinked, we can use ---
explanation: ls -i shows file names with inode numbers. Two hard linked files have the same inode number.

question: The environment variable $SHELL gives ----
options: All the shells available in the system
explanation:
If we give the command 
echo $SHELL
we will get the current shell name (most often bash)

To know the shells available in the system, you can display /etc/shells

question: The command to delete a non-empty directory is -----
explanation:
rm -r dir-name

will remove the files, subdirectories within the directory and then remove the directory itself.

e.g.
To delete the directory /home/David/Downloads/AllPhotos with all its contents, you can give the command

rm -r /home/David/Downloads/AllPhots

question: The command pstree displays
options: All running process in  a tree structure
All files in directory in a tree structure

question: The command ctrl+alt+F4 -----
explanation:
The first 6 virtual terminals tty1 to tty6 can be opened using the key combinations
ctr+alt+f1 to f6

ctrl+alt+f7 is used to switch to GUI terminal

question: The directory /proc/200 is
options: The home directory of user with uid 200
Virtual directory with information of process 200

question: To execute commands one after another, they can be written in same line separated by ----- character
explanation:
To given multiple commands in the same line, you should separate these commands using semicolon.

e.g.

cd ~/myscripts;cat a.sh;chmod u+x a.sh

This will execute the 3 commands specified one after another.

question: To give read,write and execute permission to owner-user and no permission to group and others for a file temp, we should use the command
explanation:
The permission bits are as follows

user      group   other 
rwx         rwx      rwx

1 for each bit indicates permission and 0 indicates no permission

So in the example below

111 000 000

will mean read, write and execute permission to user and no permission to group and others. 

Writing the value in octal, we have 0700

question:
What does the following command do?
ls>/dev/null
options: Displays the list of all files in working directory
Appends the listing of working directory to device null
Discards the output of ls command
explanation: By sending the output of any command /dev/null, it is completely discarded

question: What does the operator >> do?
options: It sends the output of a command to a file
It takes input of a command from a file
It appends output of a command to a file

question: Which of the following commands take us to our home directory
explanation:
The environment variable $HOME is the path of the home directory.

~ symbol in path indicates home directory

Also cd without any parameters takes us to our home directory.

question: How do we exit from shell?
explanation: ctrl D closes the shell. So does the exit command. Of course you can press the close command of the shell window too.

question: The command to unmount a file system is
explanation: The command to unmount a drive is umount, not unmount

question: The command to display last 10 commands used is
explanation:
History command displays all commands in the history.

history n will display last n previous commands given to the bash

question:
If two files are hardlinked, then they have same inode number. 
True or false?
explanation:
A hardlink is a file that points to the same underlying inode, as another file. So when a hard link is created this link points to the same physical file. 

ls -l shows the number of links to a file. When a hard link is removed, the number of links to the file is reduced by 1. When the number of links is zero, then the file is physically deleted.

question: How do you list all processes which are running bash command?
explanation:
ps -C command

displays all processes which are running the command

e.g.

ps -C gedit
displays all processes which are running the gedit editor.

question: How do you find what is your current shell?
explanation: The environment variable $SHELL gives the name of current shell.

question: Where is the history of commands you have used stored?
explanation:
The history of commands are stored in the home directory as a hidden file .bash_history.

You can cat it or use history command to see the history. up arrow command also browses through the previous commands.

question: What command can be used to stop a running process?
explanation:
SIGKILL will kill a process with force :)

9 is the signal number of SIGKILL

And kill command is used to send a signal to a process.

question: The address http://127.0.0.1 is the address of
Bash scripts start with a line called shebang which is
explanation:
shebang is number sign followed by exclamatory sign.

Most shell scripts begin with shebang followed by /bin/bash

It gives the absolute path of bash interpreter. The script is executed using this command.

question: The full form of GNU is -----------
Which command displays who is logged in and what they are doing?
explanation:
who command gives a list of people logged to the system.

w gives more information. It gives the user name, the terminal they are logged from, what command they are executing and the login time

question: Which of the following is not a valid command in Linux?
explanation:
w is a command which give the users logged in and what they are doing.

X starts the X server (display server)

question: A script file can only be run, after using this command.
explanation:
In order for any file to be executed, it must have execute permission for the current user. So after writing the script, you must use

chmod u+x script-file-name

If you want the script to be executable by all users, you can also give the command

chmod a+x script-file-name

question: The command which gives a short description of a command is -----
explanation:
whatis gives a short description of command or program. 

which displays the path of a command 

e.g.
$whatis firefox
firefox (1)          - a free and open source web browser from Mozilla
 $ which firefox
/usr/bin/firefox

question: What does which command do?
Which process is having the process id of 1?
explanation:
Init is a daemon process that continues running until the system is shut down.

 It is the direct or indirect ancestor of all other processes and automatically adopts all orphaned processes.

 Init is started by the kernel during the booting process; a kernel panic will occur if the kernel is unable to start it.

question: Which command shows the running processes in a hierarchical tree format?
The command ---- will execute the third command in your command history
explanation:
! followed by - followed by n will execute the previous nth command from history.

e.g.

ls
cat a.txt
date
!-2

Now last command will execute -2nd command which is cat a.txt

question: Which command displays exit status of previous command?
Which command copies the entire directory including sub-directories?
explanation: cp -r will copy the directory content recursively including subdirectories and their subdirectories

question: If I am in /bin directory and I want to go to my home directory. Which command should I use?
In which directory is Linux Kernel file vmlinuz file present?
explanation: /boot directory contains all boot related data and info files including the kernel. The kernel file will have a name vmlinuz followed by version number.

question: What does the following command do?
options: Displays the contents of the file
Displays the file in octal form
Displays the file in hexadecimal form
explanation:
od command dumps the file in octal or other formats.

od -x dumps it in hex
od -b dumps the file in bytes. 
od -a shows the file as named characters.

question: Which of the following operating systems is not based on Linux?
explanation: BSD is a unix operating system developed by Berkeley Software designs. It is not a Linux version.

question: How do you hide a file in Linux?
explanation:
In Linux, any file whose name starts with a period is hidden. 

In order to hide a file, you just need to add a dot at the begining of the file. 

To display all files including hidden files, you should use -a option with ls command.

question: Which command can be used in place of echo command?
explanation:
printf is used for displaying information in scripts. But the difference is printf needs format specifier similar to printf of C language. 

printf "Hello world"
printf "%d" 12

question: How do you list all files with u or v in the third letter of filename?
explanation:
? in a file name stands for one unknown character. And set of characters in square brackets is a regular expression which takes any one of them

e.g.
$ls
one.c  ong.sh  other.sh b.sh ond.c
$ls ??on[eg].*
one.c ong.sh

question:
The command
touch a.txt -------
options: creates file or changes its timestamp if it already exists.
explanation: The touch command is used to create new, empty files. It is also used to change the timestamps   on existing files and directories.

question: Which of the following statements is false?
options: cd ~ takes you to user's home directory
cd .. takes you to parent directory
cd takes you to user's home directory

question: Which command shows the user name?
explanation:
The envirnoment variable $USER also has current user name.

echo $USER
can also be used to display current user name

question: How do you send errors of a command to a file?
explanation:
command > filename sends the output of the command to file.

command 2>filename sends errors to file.

e.g.
ls absentfile 2>errfile
 cat errfile
ls: cannot access 'absentfile': No such file or directory

question: How do you display only empty lines of a file using grep command?
What does the variable $# contain in a shell script?
explanation: $@ gives the complete list of command line parameters and $# gives the count.

question: What does the folloing command do?
options: Reduces the size of the file
Displays the file one page at a time
explanation:
Both "less" and "more" commands display the contents of a file one page at a time.

more lets us scroll down only. But less lets us scroll the file both up and down.

question: How do you make a file readable to everyone, writeable to owner and executable to owner and group?
explanation:
You can even write multiple commands like this

chmod +r filename;chmod u+w  filename;chmod ug+x filename

question: To know the type of file - whether it is a device file, text file, executable file etc., you should use ---- command.
explanation:
file command determines the type of file.

e.g.

$file one.c
one.c: C source, ASCII text
 $ file pos.sh
pos.sh: Bourne-Again shell script, ASCII text executable

question: Which command is used to see the contents of a file?
explanation: cat displays content of file. more and less display the content of a file one page at a time.

question: #! is called ----
explanation: Most of shell scripts begin with shebang. Shebang tells the system to use the file specified in the line for running the script.

question: Which of the following is a Linux shell?
To test a condition in  a script, the condition has to be enclosed in a -----
explanation:
The conditions in a shell script are to be written in square brackets and there must be spaces between brackets and conditions.

e.g.

#!/bin/bash
a=12
if [ $a -eq 10 ]; then
   echo "equal to 10"
else
   echo "not equal to 10"
fi

question: Which of the statements is correct?
options: echo prints the content followed by a newline character
printf prints the content followed by a newline
Both echo and printf print content followed by newline
explanation: printf can be used in a script to print formatted output. But printf does not print a newline character automatically. You should add \n in the string to print a newline character.

question: We have written a script. Complete it to print the value of variable a.
explanation: A variable can be bare only we are defining it. In all other uses, it has to be preceeded by a $ sign.

question: How do you write a comment in a script?
explanation: A comment in script is written by starting the line with #

question: Which of the following statements are true?
options: Every line in script must end with a ;
Every variable in script must be defined with its data type

question: How do you list only the file abc.txt?
What does the command tty do?
options: Displays the type of terminal in use
Changes the terminal to the next one

question: What is the command to display only hidden files?
explanation:
regular expression with ^ searches for lines which begin with pattern given after ^.

By giving ^. we are searching for files which begin with . - so only hidden files are searched.

question: The difference betwen cmp and diff commands is
options: diff compares the files line by line and cmp compares the files byte by byte
diff compares the files byte by byte and cmp compares the files line by line
There is no difference between the two commands

question: Which command copies the input from standard input device to both standard output and files?
explanation:
By giving tee command, input from console is copied to standard output as well as file.

e.g.
tee one.txt
hello world
hello world
goodbye world
goodbye world
ctrl d

Now the two lines hello world and goodbye world are copied to file one.txt and they are also displayed on screen.

question: What is the command to print all elements of an array?
Which command can be used to find how long the system has been running?
How do you define an array in a shell script
explanation:
Array elements are defined within brackets.

a[0]=11
a[1]=20

Like other variables, there is no need to define the array beforehand.

Another way of defining arrays is we can assign individual elements. 

a={11 12 13}

To access individual elements of an array, we can use syntax shown below

${a[0]}
${a[1]} etc.

${a[@]} gives entire array

question: What is the output of this shell script?
Core of the Linux operating system is called ----
Command interpreter in Linux is ----
A zombie process is the one   -------
explanation:
A dead process is cleaned up by its parent. 

When a process is dead, but the parent is busy, then the process becomes zombie until the parent reaps it.

question: What does fork() system call return in parent process
explanation: fork() system call creates a child process which is a replica of parent. fork() returns child process id in parent and 0 in child process.

question: Which of the following statements are true?
options: chown command changes the permissions of a file
chown command changes the owner and/or group of the file
explanation:
chown command changes the owner of a file. 

e.g.

chown Drew ~/abc.txt

Now Drew is the new owner of the file abc.txt

chown Drew:Drew ~/scripts/*

All files in scripts folder are owned by Drew and belong to group Drew.

question: Which command in vi editor copies current line to the buffer?
explanation:
yy yanks a line or multiple lines and saves them in buffer. 

These lines can be pasted to cursor location by p

question: Command to delete the current line in vi editor is ----

options: vi editor commands are not case sensitive
esc key toggles between insert mode and command mode
:s command is used to save the current file

question: Which command is used to set terminal characterstics?

explanation: s in permission  is suid bit. When a program has suid bit set, then the user who executes the program will have same permssion as the owner of the file.

question: The permission bits of /usr/bin/passwd has s bit set because ----
options: it should allow users to execute it without sudo
it should allow ordinary users to write to it
explanation: s in permission  is suid bit. When a program has suid bit set, then the user who executes the program will have same permssion as the owner of the file.

question: What does chmod +t do?
options: It sets the effective userid for execution
It sets effective group id on execution
explanation:
Sticky Bit is mainly used on folders in order to avoid deletion of a folder and it’s content by other users though they having write permissions on the folder contents. 

If Sticky bit is enabled on a folder, the folder contents are deleted by only owner who created them. No one else can delete other users data in this folder.

sticky bit is set using +t in chmod command

question: Which of the following commands changes the modification date of a file?
The command to extract a column from a text file is ----
explanation:
cut cuts the columns from a given file and displays it. It takes tab as the delimiter. You can specify other delimiters by using -d option. 

e.g.

cut -d " " -f 2 myfile.txt
cuts the 2nd column of myfile.txt

If myfile.txt is 
one two three four five
nine eight seven six

then the output of cut command is 
two
eight

question: In which programming language are the system calls in Linux are written?

Write the command to display inode numbers of all files in the working directory
explanation: -i in ls command displays the inode numbes.

question: When a new directory is created, how many links does it have?
explanation: It has a link to the parent and to itself.

question: What does the command du do?
options: Displays the content of current directory
Displays the disk usage of a directory
explanation:
du - disk usage command shows the number of blocks used by directories and files

e.g.

du ~/scripts
52


du ~/scripts
52K

-h flag is for human readable form. 
 
du ~/scripts/onesorted.c  -h
4.0K	/home/usha/scripts/onesorted.c

grep echo one.sh

echo ${b[0]}
echo "All elements of b are ${b[@]}"

grep is displaying all lines in one.sh file which contain the text echo

question: The command to specify  permissions for newly created files is -----
What is the process id of init process?
explanation:
In Linux init (short for initialization) is the first process started during booting of the computer system. Init is a daemon process that continues running until the system is shut down. 

It is the direct or indirect ancestor of all other processes and automatically adopts all orphaned processes. 

Init is started by the kernel during the booting process

question: How do you display last five lines of a file?
explanation:
head and tail commands are used to display the first 10 lines and last 10 lines of a file respectively.

-n number
can be used in these to specify the number of lines to be displayed.

question: Which command can be used to display all the processes by a user?

explanation: /etc/group file contains the groups in the system and the users in each group.

question: The file which has the list of groups and its members is

To autocomplete commands in a linux terminal, you should use the following key.
Command id shows ---
In the /proc file system, each of the numbered sub-directories correspond to ----
options: Each running process in the system
explanation: In /proc, there is a sub-directory corresponding to each running process. The subdirectory name is the process id. In this sub-directory information about the process is present - like command line, open files, memory etc.

question: What is the total number of signals available to be used?
explanation:
There are 32 signals. These can be listed using the command 

kill -l

question: The command ps -t ? will display ----
explanation:
ps -t terminal 
displays all processes belonging to a terminal. But ps -t ? displays all processes not belonging to any terminal - i.e. daemon processes.

question:

3) /boot has kernel and boot files

options: Only root can add new users.
A user can be added using adduser command
Password of himself can be modified by any user without knowing previous password
explanation:
A user can change his/her password after providing the current password.

But root user can change anyone's password without knowing the current password.

question: The shell name bash stand for ----
The system wide commands and environmental variables are stored in the file-----
explanation: .profile and .bashrc are used for initializing individual users. These are stored in users' home directories. Systme wide settings like aliases, environment variables etc. are available in /etc/profile and /etc/bash.bashrc

question: Command umask is used for ----
options: Using the permissions specified in mask
unsetting the permissions in mask command
explanation:
If umask is 0022 then, the newly created files will have permissions 0755 i.e. there will r,w and x permission for owner, r and x for group and others. 

So the bits specified in umask are unset in the permission.

question: What will kill -SIGTERM -1 command do?
options: Kills all processes with your UID

renice is used for changing the priority of running process.

renice 12 1608

explanation: 1 saves and quits. 2 quits without saving

question: If the umask is set to 0077, then the newly created file will have the following permission -----
explanation: The newly created file will have no permissions to others and groups

question: How do you list all the links in /etc directory
explanation: grep "^l" selects the lines which begin with l - links

question: Which command gives you the network interfaces and their configurations in your system?
explanation:
ifconfig is dated command. The newer command is ip addr

ip addr show eth0 
for example displays setting of ethernet connection

Displays all aliases defined in system
explanation:
alias command lets you define your own name for a command or sequence of commands.

e.g.

alias dir=ls

Now you can use dir command to list the files. 

Often the aliases are defined .bashrc in users' home directory.

question: What does echo command do?
options: It prints a string of text
It shows all files in the current directory
explanation:
echo command is used in shell script to display a variable

e.g.

a=1

a is 1

It gets a file from a URL
explanation: ping command

question:
What does the following command do?

shutdown -h +10
options: It shuts down the system immediately
It reboots the system after 10 minutes
It shuts down the system after 10 minutes
explanation:
shutdown with option -h shuts down the system. shutdown with option -r reboots the system.

+n will shutdown after n minutes

question: What command do you use to search for a file in linux system?

./abc.sh one "two three" four

explanation:
Variable names are prefixed with $ in a script except when defining them

$((a=$a+10))
